questions.q: Which Mughal emperor did Sher Shah Suri defeat to seize power?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri defeated Humayun and drove him into exile.

questions.q: Which dynasty did Sher Shah Suri found?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah founded the Sur dynasty, ruling northern India from 1540.

questions.q: Over what years did Sher Shah Suri rule northern India?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah ruled from 1540 to 1545 CE.

questions.q: What was Sher Shah's ethnic background?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri was an Afghan ruler of the Sur dynasty.

questions.q: Which new silver coin did Sher Shah introduce?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah introduced the rupiya, the silver coin ancestral to the modern rupee.

questions.q: Sher Shah is famous for building or restoring which great road network?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah is credited with building and improving the Grand Trunk Road across north India.

questions.q: Sher Shah Suri is best remembered as which kind of ruler?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah is remembered as a brilliant administrator who reformed revenue and governance.

questions.q: What was Sher Shah's original given name before he rose to power?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah was born Farid Khan and earned the title 'Sher Khan' for his courage.

questions.q: What does the title 'Sher' in Sher Shah mean?
questions.explanation: 'Sher' means lion or tiger; he reputedly earned it by killing a tiger single-handed.

questions.q: Sher Shah's reign briefly interrupted the rule of which empire?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah's Sur dynasty interrupted Mughal rule between Humayun's two reigns.

questions.q: At which 1539 battle did Sher Shah first defeat Humayun?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah defeated Humayun at the Battle of Chausa in 1539.

questions.q: At which 1540 battle did Sher Shah decisively defeat Humayun, ending his rule?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Kannauj in 1540 drove Humayun from India and gave Sher Shah the empire.

questions.q: Sher Shah's grand octagonal tomb stands amid a lake at which town in Bihar?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah's magnificent tomb stands in a lake at Sasaram in Bihar.

questions.q: Sher Shah's land-revenue reforms influenced the later Mughal system of which Akbar-era minister?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah's revenue measures, including land surveys, influenced Raja Todar Mal's Mughal reforms.

questions.q: Along his roads, Sher Shah built numerous rest-houses for travellers known as what?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah built sarais (caravanserais) along his highways to shelter travellers and traders.

questions.q: Sher Shah rose to power after first serving and then displacing the rulers of which region?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah built his base in Bihar and Bengal before challenging Humayun for the empire.

questions.q: Sher Shah established an efficient postal system using which mode of relay?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah organised a horse-relay postal service using stations along his road network.

questions.q: The Purana Qila (Old Fort) in Delhi is associated with Sher Shah and which feature within it?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah is linked to the Purana Qila in Delhi, which contains the Qila-i-Kuhna Mosque.

questions.q: How did Sher Shah Suri die in 1545?
options: In a gunpowder explosion during the siege of Kalinjar
questions.explanation: Sher Shah died in 1545 from a gunpowder explosion while besieging the fort of Kalinjar.

questions.q: Who succeeded Sher Shah Suri after his death in 1545?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah was succeeded by his son Islam Shah Suri (Jalal Khan).

questions.q: Sher Shah standardised land measurement and fixed the state's share of produce, often as what fraction?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah fixed the land revenue at roughly one-third of the produce, assessed by survey.

questions.q: The Grand Trunk Road that Sher Shah developed ran, in his era, from Sonargaon in Bengal to roughly where in the northwest?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah's road, the Sadak-e-Azam, ran from Sonargaon in Bengal to the northwest toward the Indus and beyond.

questions.q: Which Rajput fort did Sher Shah capture by treachery in 1543, breaking a sworn promise to its defenders?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah took the fort of Raisin in 1543, an episode marred by his violation of a safe-conduct pledge.

questions.q: At the Battle of Chausa, Sher Shah reportedly used a ruse to defeat Humayun; what was its nature?
options: Hiding war elephants in a forest
Feigning peace talks while preparing a surprise night attack
questions.explanation: At Chausa, Sher Shah lulled Humayun with negotiations, then launched a devastating surprise attack.

questions.q: Sher Shah's coins were notable for being struck with consistent weight and purity in which three metals?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah issued standardised silver rupiya, gold mohur, and copper dam coins.

questions.q: Which Rajput-held fort was Sher Shah besieging when he was killed in the 1545 explosion?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah was killed by an exploding rocket or magazine while besieging the fort of Kalinjar in 1545.

questions.q: Sher Shah's administration issued documents recording each peasant's rights and dues, called by which names?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah gave cultivators a patta (title deed) and took a qabuliyat (deed of agreement) fixing their dues.

questions.q: Sher Shah divided his empire into administrative units; the larger of these was known as what?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah organised his realm into sarkars, each subdivided into parganas, a structure the Mughals later adapted.

questions.q: A Persian biography of Sher Shah, an important source on his reign, is the Tarikh-i-Sher Shahi written by whom?
questions.explanation: Abbas Khan Sarwani wrote the Tarikh-i-Sher Shahi, a key account of Sher Shah's life and administration.

questions.q: The Sur dynasty's collapse, which allowed Humayun's return, was driven largely by what after Islam Shah's death?
options: Civil war among rival Sur claimants
A great famine in Bengal alone
questions.explanation: After Islam Shah died, the Sur dynasty fractured into warring claimants, enabling Humayun to reconquer Delhi in 1555.
